Ashik Ali Bhai Lal Ji, my father, was a renowned personality, who after migration, went through many hardships and therefore started singing ‘Qawwalis’ (Sufi songs). We are the 19th generation of Bhai Mardana. We earn living through labour work. During festive congregations, when visitors come to Pakistan, we perform ‘Keertan’. We are trying our best not to quit this tradition. We seek Guru Nanak’s blessings to continue.

ga(n)g banaaras hi(n)dhooaa(n) makaa kaabaa musalamaane || ghar ghar baabaa gaaveeaai vajan taal miradha(n)g rabaabaa || jaahar peer jagat gur baabaa || (Bhai Gurdas)

River Ganga at Benares is revered by Hindus. Muslims revere the ‘Kabba’ in Mecca. Universally, Baba’s (Guru Nanak) message of Oneness is being sung in accompaniment of drums and rababs. Baba (Guru Nanak) is revealed as a spiritual teacher for all. (Bhai Gurdas)

This visit has deeply saddened me with the present conditions of the descendants of the ‘Rababi’ tradition. Their forefathers were soulful preserves of Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana’s legacy.

To get a glimpse of individuals in Lahore who continue to carry forward the light of Guru Nanak, we visit Dr. Asma Qadri, a Professor at the Oriental College in the University of Punjab.

Dr. Asma Qadri Welcome.

Amardeep Singh Salaam (Islamic greeting), respected Asma.

Dr. Asma Qadri Sat Sri Akal (Sikh greeting). How are you?

Amardeep Singh Salaam (Islamic greeting) and Sat Sri Akal (Sikh greeting). How are you?

Dr. Asma Qadri All is well.

Amardeep Singh Thank you for your time.

Dr. Asma Qadri: No problems. You are the one travelling with time. This meeting has been pre-ordained. It is making us transcend through time. Before you arrived, I received this new book, ‘Gaavan Sang’, which I opened and Nanak’s verse came in front and you entered. jau tau prem khelan kaa chaau || sir dhar talee galee meree aau || eit maarag pair dhareejai || sir dheejai kaan na keejai ||

Guru Nanak

If you desire to play the game of love then step on the path with your ego placed on the palm. Once you step on this path, in giving up the ego, do not hesitate.

Guru Nanak

Amardeep Singh Asma Ji, I am curious as to know about your connection with Guru Nanak.

Dr. Asma Qadri Our classical poetry, it all starts from Baba Farid followed by Sultan Bahu, Sachal Sarmast, Shah Hussain, Bullhe Shah, Waris Shah, Damodar, Mian Muhammad Bakhsh, Maulvi Gulam Rasool Alam Poori. We have read all these poets in the congregation with Najam Sahib. For the last 40-45 years this process is ongoing, every week. There itself, we have been reading the ‘Granth Sahib’ (Sikh scripture) and gaining its wisdom within us. The tradition before Baba Nanak is that of Baba Farid. What was the linkage of our poets with Baba Nanak? You will be surprised to learn. In our Sindh, there was one poet, Sachal. A new poet, Yusuf came to him. Sachal read his poetry, and renamed him ‘Nanak Yusuf’. So in Sindh, the works of ‘Nanak Yusuf’, the poet, are still there. We have studied his works in the sangat of Najam Hussain Sahib. In the sangat of Najam Hussain Syed, we are now reading and understanding ‘Granth Sahib’ (Sikh scripture) for the fourth time. With us, even today, even at this time, within Lahore, all this is ongoing. Nanak is alive. He is present. The seeds which he sowed, they are sprouting. Every seed has two parts within but only one plant emerges from it. This is the wisdom to awaken consciousness which has been sowed as seeds by Nanak.

Amardeep Singh Although 70 years post partition, a lot of our heritage has faded, yet within Lahore, the links are still present. Seeing this one feels elated. Baba (Guru Nanak) is still present here. You all have nurtured him.

Spirituality is beyond the realms of organized belief systems, best defined as a free flow of personal inquiry into diverse philosophies.

so kiau visarai meree mai ||

Raag Asa, Guru Nanak

Why do you forget , O my Soul?

Raag Asa, Guru Nanak

Saleema Khawaja, a lawyer by profession is one such soul who got attracted to Guru Nanak’s philosophy by listening to the late Rababi, Bhai Ghulam Muhammad Chand, son of late Bhai Chand, one of the last Rababi’s to perform ‘Keertan’ at the Darbar Sahib, the Golden Temple, at Amritsar, in India.

aakhaa jeevaa visarai mar jaau || aakhan aaukhaa saachaa naau ||

Raag Asa, Guru Nanak

When I contemplate, I remain alive, forgetting, I die. Acquiring true awareness is difficult.

Raag Asa, Guru Nanak

Saleema Khawaja It started somewhat like this. I met Bhai Chand. He then came to our home and sang ‘Shabads’ (Verses from Sikh scripture). I was drawn to that. The way he was singing that. There was a pull. A definite pull. There I realized the expanse of Baba Nanak’s verses. In the year 2007, I started learning from him. Embodiment of that tradition was Bhai Chand and he imbibed from his elders. Through oral tradition, this heritage has been passed on and somehow has reached me. I am very fortunate.

galee jog na hoiee || ek dhirasaT kar samasar jaanai jogee kaheeaai soiee ||

Raag Suhi, Guru Nanak

By mere words, the union is not attained. Those who look upon all as one, only they can be known as spiritually enlightened.

Raag Suhi, Guru Nanak

I am reminded of a saying by a Japanese scholar, ‘Beholding the wise in reverence will not make one wise. What they sought, does.’

From Lahore in Pakistan we cross the India-Pakistan border at Wagah and resume exploring the life journey of Guru Nanak from Sultanpur Lodhi in India. In travelling, I reflect on the true meaning of ‘sangat’, congregational learning. It is indeed a practical way in which these few people in Lahore continue to seek beyond the boundaries of faiths.

saahib meraa eko hai || eko hai bhaiee eko hai ||

Raag Asa, Guru Nanak

My master is One. It is one, O fellow, it is one.

Raag Asa, Guru Nanak
For the conversation

Discussion Pointers

Episode 2: Shafaf Khayal (Clear Thought)

These discussion pointers offer pathways to explore the rich historical and philosophical content about Guru Nanak's life and his spiritual imports. The questions aim to stimulate deeper understanding of both the historical context that shaped his pursuit and the enduring philosophical insights that emerged from his experiences in Sultanpur Lodhi and beyond.

Historical Discussion Pointers:

1. What was the significance of Sultanpur Lodhi in Guru Nanak's formative years? The episode reveals that Sultanpur Lodhi played a crucial role in Guru Nanak's early adulthood. It was here that Guru Nanak was appointed as a 'Modi' (supervisor of the state granary) by Governor Daulat Khan Lodhi. This city, situated on the eastern bank of the River Bein, was where Guru Nanak settled into married life, had two sons (Sri Chand and Lakhmi Chand), and was recognized as an honest, compassionate official. How could the diverse urban environment, with its harmonious blend of mixed communities, have shaped Guru Nanak’s evolving perspective on the world?

2. How did Guru Nanak's approach to his official duties at the Modikhana reflect his emerging spiritual values? As supervisor of the state granary, Guru Nanak demonstrated exceptional integrity in a corrupt system. The episode states that Guru Nanak, an adherent of honesty, sincerely performed his duties. For the underprivileged, Guru Nanak would distribute an incremental quota of grain. When accused of mismanagement, an inspection found his accounts "in order and a surplus amount of grains. How did this practical application of ethics reflect the foundations of his revolutionary messages?

3. What was the significance of Guru Nanak's three-day disappearance in the River Bein? The episode describes a pivotal mystical experience: One morning, when Guru Nanak went to bathe, he did not emerge from the River Bein and after three days he was seen coming out of the River Bein, about 3 kilometers away from the point he had entered. After this experience, he was endowed with clarity that he wanted to share widely. What could this transformative experience reveal about his profound understanding of the essence of life?

4. How did individuals become attracted to Guru Nanak's way of life, and what does this tell us about the growing influence of his philosophy? The episode mentions several early adherents: Bhagirath, chief of the village of Malsihan, who was deeply impressed by the new 'Modi'; Bhai Mardana, who came from Talwandi seeking Guru Nanak; and Mansukh, a noted trader in the city, who joined after hearing about the revolutionary thoughts and actions of Guru Nanak. What does the diverse background of these early adherents suggest about Guru Nanak's appeal?

5. How did Guru Nanak respond to challenges from religious authorities? When questioned about his radical statements by the Qazi and summoned before Daulat Khan Lodhi, Guru Nanak maintained that the most honourable religion is that of practicing righteousness. When invited to join the Muslim prayer, he agreed but pointed out the Qazi's wandering thoughts during prayer. In what ways did these encounters demonstrate Guru Nanak's innovative approach to challenging established religious authorities?

6. What has been the legacy of Bhai Mardana and the Rababi tradition across centuries? The episode reveals the background of Bhai Mardana, Guru Nanak's spiritual companion, who accompanied him with his Rabab (stringed instrument). His progeny established a tradition of Muslim bards known as 'Rababis' who sang Gurbani (verses from Sikh scripture). The partition of 1947 severely impacted this centuries-old tradition; however, descendants of Bhai Mardana in Lahore, claiming to be the ‘18th and 19th generation,’ are continuing this legacy despite hardships. How does this musical tradition reflect the interfaith dimensions of Guru Nanak's message?

Philosophical Discussion Pointers:

1. How does Guru Nanak's declaration of equality challenge social hierarchies? The episode quotes Guru Nanak: no one is high or low. His proclamation that all are equal, fundamentally rejected distinctions based on religion, caste, colour and gender. How revolutionary was this stance in a society structured around hierarchical divisions?

2. What does Guru Nanak mention about the relationship between honesty and spirituality? Guru Nanak states: hear the true instructions of the conscience. The ledger of one's actions is accountable by one's consciousness. How does this verse connect ethical conduct with spiritual awareness?

3. How does Guru Nanak define true religion beyond ritualistic practices? When questioned by the Qazi, Guru Nanak maintained that Righteousness is the only truthful universal law. He stated that he follows the path that leads to Divine Consciousness, which does not see anyone as a stranger and has no religion. What implications does this position have for traditional religious boundaries?

4. How does Guru Nanak describe the challenges of the mind in spiritual seeking? Guru Nanak acknowledges that the mind remains astray, in doubt between attachment and detachment. It is extremely enticed and lured by poisonous temptations. How does this understanding of human psychology inform his spiritual insights?

5. What is the significance of Oneness in Guru Nanak's spiritual vision? The episode concludes with Guru Nanak's verse: My master is One. It is one. Throughout the episode, there are references to his message of Oneness and philosophy of Oneness. How does this concept of unity extend beyond theological monotheism to social and spiritual unity?

6. How does Guru Nanak's concept of self-illumination relate to his approach to truth? Guru Nanak states that Spiritual knowledge is the gem through which the self illuminates. This appears in the context of questioning how to attain truth without having to wander physically. What does this suggest about Guru Nanak's view on the internal versus external sources of spiritual insight?

YouTube captions — spoken narration only (no speaker labels or on-screen verses). Click a timestamp to open the video at that point.

0:00:05‘Shafaf Khayal’, ‘Clear Thought’, presents the youthful years of the compassionate defiant who endowed the clarity of unity in diversity. Embodiment of that tradition was Bhai Chand and he imbibed from his elders.

0:00:39It also explores the legacy of Bhai Mardana, the lifelong friend of Guru Nanak. Guru Nanak establishes that all are equal.

0:02:39Guru Nanak's elder sister Bebe Nanaki was married to Jai Ram. The couple lived in Sultanpur Lodhi, a town on the eastern bank of the River Bein. The city holds immense importance in the context of the life events of Guru Nanak. In present times, due to political reasons, the India-Pakistan border can only be crossed at a few designated points.

0:03:08To have insight into the formative years of his adulthood, we visit Sultanpur Lodhi in India through Wagah border. During Guru Nanak's time, this city was under the governance of Daulat Khan Lodhi, a Muslim Governor of the Lodhi dynasty.

0:03:35Both, Hindu and Muslim communities were then an integral part of the social fabric of this city. Concerned about her brother's future, who was still not showing any interest in worldly affairs, Bebe Nanaki and Jai Ram explored the idea of Guru Nanak relocating to Sultanpur Lodhi. Hoping a change of place may influence Guru Nanak's frame of mind, his parents agreed that he moved to Sultanpur Lodhi.

0:04:10In Sultanpur Lodhi, we visit the place which is believed to have been the home of Bebe Nanaki and Jai Ram. Jai Ram was a high ranking official, the chief executive of the 'Modi Khana', the State Granary at Sultanpur Lodhi, Jai ram recommended Guru Nanak's name for a job at the ‘Modi Khana’ (State Granary).

0:04:40Daulat Khan Lodhi appointed Guru Nanak as a 'Modi', a position of immense authority entrusting him with supervising the state granary, keeping daily records of the commodities and maintaining the ledger. Guru Nanak, now having settled with a job in Sultanpur Lodhi, took up a house and got Mata Sulakhni, his wife, to move from Talwandi.

0:05:14We visit the place where it is believed was once the home of Guru Nanak and Mata Sulakhni in Sultanpur Lodhi. Guru Nanak lived a householders life. Gurdwara Guru Ka Bagh marks Guru Nanak's stay in Sultanpur Lodhi with his family.

0:05:41Soon they were blessed with two sons, Sri Chand and Lakhmi Chand. Sri Chand, the elder son, chose an ascetic lifestyle, initiating the ‘Udasin’ (ascetic) order, which contributed significantly to spreading the philosophy of Guru Nanak to far and wide lands. Sri Chand passed the leadership of the 'Udasin' (ascetic) tradition to Baba Gurditta, the eldest son of Guru Hargobind, the sixth Sikh Guru.

0:06:19Lakhmi Chand, the second son of Guru Nanak led the householder's life. At a short distance is the Gurdwara Hatt Sahib, built at the site where the ‘Modi Khana’, the state granary existed. Guru Nanak, now holding a seat of authority at the 'Modi Khana' (State Granary), was exposed to the immoral ways of society.

0:06:53Corruption was entrenched in the social system. For their benefit, the rich would bribe the authorities. To take advantage, the officials would misuse their power and the ones who suffered were the weaker sections of society. Guru Nanak, an adherent of honesty, sincerely performed his duties.

0:07:18For the underprivileged, Guru Nanak would distribute an incremental quota of grain. Soon, his reputation grew far and wide. The people at the grassroots were happy with Guru Nanak, and he became popular as a hard working, trustworthy, honest and compassionate officer. A jealous co-worker threatened by Guru Nanak’s popularity complained to the Governor that Guru Nanak was an irresponsible officer who was squandering the state revenue.

0:07:53Governor Daulat Khan Lodhi ordered a tally of accounts and measurement of the quantity of grains. After a thorough inspection, the appointed officers found the accounts in order and a surplus amount of grains, which some citizens, having noticed Guru Nanak's charitable conduct, had donated for distribution to the needy. Guru Nanak draws attention to the immutable fact that no one can escape consciousness

0:09:13which has the ledger of one's deeds. As per the law of nature, falsehood perishes and truth prevails. Corruption is the exploitation of entrusted power for personal benefit. Honesty is the fulcrum for a fair democratic society leading to socio-economic equilibrium and harmony. This human rights revolution was seeded by Guru Nanak’s actions at the ‘Modi Khana’ (State Granary).

0:09:49Gurudwara Hatt Sahib is a testimony of Guru Nanak's practical implementation of practicing what he preached. Honest earning, generous sharing and spiritual contemplation. Bhagirath, the chief of the village Malsihan was a virtuous man. During his visits to deposit the grains as land revenue at the ‘Modi Khana’ (State Granary), he was deeply impressed by the new 'Modi', Guru Nanak, who was proficient in both, his job and knowledge of spirituality.

0:10:28Bhagirath started visiting Sultanpur frequently to have spiritual interactions with Guru Nanak. The adherents of Guru Nanak started from such first few like Bhagirath. The world of Guru Nanak's goodwill had spread around the region. Deeply touched by Guru Nanak's radical influence in society, Bhai Mardana, who was in Talwandi, desired to meet him.

0:10:57As chance would have it, Mehta Kalu asked Bhai Mardana to visit Sultanpur and bring back news of Guru Nanak's welfare. At Sultanpur, Bhai Mardana Stayed at Guru Nanak's home. As a routine, in the early hours, Guru Nanak would bathe in the River Bein and then both, he and Bhai Mardana would sing spiritual verses.

0:11:31One day, Bhai Mardana expressed his financial limitations to have his daughter married. On hearing by Bhai Mardana's dilemma, Guru Nanak requested Bhagirath to facilitate the wedding requirements. Bhagirath visited Lahore to make the arrangements. Here he met Mansukh, a noted trader in the city.

0:12:01On hearing about the revolutionary thoughts and actions of Guru Nanak, Mansukh joined Bhagirath on his way back to Sultanpur. Mansukh was elated at meeting Guru Nanak and became another one of his early adherents. On having received the goods for the marriage, Bhai Mardana left for Talwandi.

0:12:29Soon after getting his daughter married, he returned to Sultanpur to be with Guru Nanak. We now visit Gurdwara Ber Sahib on the banks of River Bein.

0:12:54This gurdwara is a landmark site in the heart of Sultanpur Lodhi City. The quaint scenic beauty of this area was the reason Guru Nanak would meditate here after bathing in the river. How can one attain truth without having to wander physically?

0:14:05With an abundance of preachers in the realm of organized faiths, I wonder how many have experienced the Truth referred by Guru Nanak. Gurdwara Ber Sahib represents the time Guru Nanak spent on the banks of River Bein, meditating and having discourses with Allah Ditta, a Muslim holy man.

0:14:37Time and again, saints have been proclaiming that the Creator resides in every aspect of the creation. So can pilgrimage sites provide spiritual solace to the seeker's mind? Guru Nanak message affirms the overarching importance

0:15:24of the right intentions to attain solace. I wonder, why we continue to endorse ritualism and have not yet evolved to grasp the essence of Guru Nanak's wisdom. To contemplate in seclusion, Guru Nanak would often visit the graveyard on the other side of the river.

0:15:53Guru Nanak's father-in-law, Moola Khatri, along with Sama Pandit, visited the graveyard to understand, why Guru Nanak was spending much time in isolation. Unable to comprehend the meditative state of mind, Moola Khatri assumed Guru Nanak was possessed and sought assistance from Fakirs to free him from evil spirits.

0:16:20One morning, when Guru Nanak went to bathe, he did not emerge from the River Bein. Worried by the disappearance, assuming Guru Nanak had drowned, the onlookers raised the alarm. Governor Daulat Khan Lodhi made every possible arrangement for his search, but all efforts went futile. Guru Nanak had probably swum across the river, into the dense forest, to contemplate in solitude.

0:16:52After three days, he was seen coming out of the River Bein, about three kilometers away from the point he had entered. The Gurdwara Sant Ghat is built to commemorate the emergence of Guru Nanak from the River Bein.

0:17:34Guru Nanak’s philosophy of Oneness, was pronounced by him at the banks of River Bein, after meditation and contemplation. After this mystic experience, Guru Nanak was endowed with clarity that he wanted to disseminate with one and all. Connected with the Consciousness, Guru Nanak was in awe of the Omnipresent and only saw goodness in the universe.

0:18:18Guru Nanak, was now in 'Birha', an intense state of dispassion for duality. Seeing Guru Nanak wanting to relinquish his worldly possessions, people believed he had lost his sanity. Guru Nanak stated,

0:19:46the most honorable religion is that of practicing righteousness. He fearlessly proclaimed that all are equal. On the path of spirituality, there is no space for bigotry and supremacy of any religion, caste, color and gender. This offended the custodians of the prevalent belief systems.

0:20:11Infuriated, they complained to Daulat Khan Lodhi, the Governor. Guru Nanak was summoned to give an explanation for his statement. Qazi, the arbiter of Islamic law, who supported the complaint, was also present to question Guru Nanak. He asked, if righteousness is the most honorable religion, then what is the importance of faiths, which have been established for centuries by Prophets, Seers, Sages and Bhagats (spiritual beings)?

0:20:45Guru Nanak replied that a truly religious person is one who follows the true essence of the religion. The Qazi questioned Guru Nanak, since every man of religion follows a path, Muslim or Hindu, so what is Guru Nanak's chosen direction? Guru Nanak replied that he seeks universal kingship and follows the path that leads to Divine Consciousness, which does not see anyone as a stranger and has no religion.

0:21:17Qazi, discontented with the answer, said, if you are on the path of the Divine, then join us for the 'Namaz'. the Muslim prayer. Guru Nanak agreed and accompanied them to the mosque. We visit the Gurdwara Antaryamta, which is built in the memory of the narrative of Guru Nanak being invited to read the 'Namaz'.

0:21:54While the Qazi was reciting the 'Namaz', Guru Nanak looked at him and smiled. This infuriated the Qazi, who asked the reason for his amusement. Guru Nanak replied, you were not reciting the 'Namaz' honestly. Even though you are physically present in the mosque, your mind was at home, anxious about the newborn filly. Adorning religious garb and performing rituals is easy, but the real test is practicing the path spiritual leaders subscribe.

0:22:28A faithful follower of one's faith is the one who is compassionate, humble, reposes complete faith and submits to the Divine Will. Else, the daily prayers, rituals and clothing are nothing but a false show. Even though we aspire to inculcate right actions, what hampers the aspiration?

0:23:11Illusioned by materialism and tricked by sensory attractions, the mind is subject to delusion. It causes human temperament to oscillate between attachment and detachment, which hampers the desired aspiration to act righteously. After the partition of 1947, due to a population swap based on religion, the city of Sultanpur Lodhi now has a majority Sikh and Hindu population, with the scant presence of Muslims.

0:23:50This historic mosque within the compound of the old fort is a beautiful representation of inter-faith harmony. Within its premises, the Muslims of the city have kept scriptures containing Guru Nanak's verses as memorabilia of the message of oneness. Finally, Guru Nanak decided to embark on a spiritual journey to visit places of religious, social,

0:24:31and cultural congregations. He requested Bhai Mardana to accompany him in these spiritual explorations. Both probably felt the need to incorporate music in their journey, and for this, they decided to carry a Rabab, a pluck string instrument, which has the ability to create a percussive sound along with scream vibrations.

0:25:08Words overlaid with spiritual music help transcend the thoughts of the mind. Before embarking on his journey, Guru Nanak, requested Bhai Mardana to buy a Rabab from an artisan by the name of by Bhai Phiranda. We now travel from Sultanpur Lodhi to Bharoana Village in the basin where the rivulet Bein joins the Beas River.

0:25:58This gurdwara is dedicated to Bhai Phiranda, an artisan who presented a Rabab to Bhai Mardana. After Bhai Mardana procured the Rabab, Bhai Phiranda accompanied him to personally hand over the instrument to Guru Nanak in Sultanpur Lodhi. In ecstasy, Guru Nanak sang.

0:27:41Guru Nanak, in his humility, sees himself now prepared to undertake a mission. Having fulfilled his fatherly duty, Bhai Mardana was also ready to be a part of Guru Nanak's mission. Bhai Gurdas, in his writings, mentions.

0:28:14Historically, Muslim bards who sang ‘Gurbani’, the verses from Guru Granth Sahib (scripture), came to be known as ‘Rababis’. For centuries, they sang spiritual verses in the Gurdwaras. The partition in 1947 divided the subcontinent in two sovereign nations, India and Pakistan.

0:28:40Unfortunately, this political event not only divided the geography, but also adversely impacted many intertwined cultural aspects, including the ‘Rababi’ (Muslim Bards) tradition. In presents times, due to political reasons, the India-Pakistan border can only be crossed at a few designated points. We now make a detour from the footsteps of Guru Nanak and travel to Lahore in Pakistan through Wagah border to connect with those strands who are keeping the legacy alive.

0:29:45We visit Mohalla Hussianpura in Lahore where reside the family of the Rababis Mohammad Hussain Lal, Naeem Tahir Lal and Sarfraz Hussain Lal, who claim to be the 18th and 19th generation descendants of Bhai Mardana. This visit has deeply saddened me

0:33:37with the present conditions of the descendants of the ‘Rababi’ tradition. Their forefathers were soulful preservers of Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana's legacy. To get a glimpse of individuals in Lahore who continue to carry forward the light of Guru Nanak, we visit Dr. Asma Qadri, a professor at the Oriental College in the University of Punjab.

0:35:19Asma Ji, I am curious to know about your connection with Guru Nanak. Spirituality is beyond the realms of organized belief systems,

0:37:31best defined as a free flow of personal inquiry into diverse philosophies. Saleema Khawaja, a lawyer by profession, is one such soul who got attracted to Guru Nanak's philosophy by listening to the late Rababi, Bhai Ghulam Muhammad Chand, son of late Bhai Chand, one of the last Rababi’s to perform ‘Keertan’ (singing) at the Darbar Sahib,

0:38:17the Golden Temple, at Amritsar, in India. I am reminded of a saying by a Japanese scholar,

0:40:24beholding the wise in reverence will not make one wise. What they sought, does. From Lahore in Pakistan, we cross the India-Pakistan border at Wagah and resume exploring the life journey of Guru Nanak from Sultanpur Lodhi in India In travelling, I reflect on the true meaning of 'sangat', the congregational learning.

0:40:52It is indeed a practical way in which these few people in Lahore continue to seek beyond the boundaries of faiths.

03 Ruhani Rawangi Spiritual Departure The onset of the first odyssey to spread Tawheed — Oneness. 41:01›
▶ Watch ↓ Script PDF

‘Ruhani Rawangi’, ‘Spiritual Departure’, presents the onset of the keen beholder’s first odyssey to observe, assimilate and disseminate ‘Tawheed’, Oneness. Guru Nanak intermingles with mystics at the seat of ‘Shakarganj’.

jaagahu jaagahu sootiho chaliaa vanajaaraa ||

Raag Asa Kafi, Guru Nanak

Awaken, Awaken! O people in slumber, the merchant is departing.

Raag Asa Kafi, Guru Nanak

In 1504 AD, at the age of 35 years, Guru Nanak embarks on a journey to awaken the soul of society from the slumber of ignorance.

Before leaving Sultanpur Lodhi, Guru Nanak visits his sister Bebe Nanaki, requesting her to look out for his family while he is away. With the concurrence of Bebe Nanaki and his wife, Mata Sulakhni, Guru Nanak, along with Bhai Mardana depart from Sultanpur Lodhi to Saidpur, a city which is now known as Eminabad.

We now travel from Sultanpur Lodhi in India to visit Eminabad in Pakistan to explore Guru Nanak’s narrative in this city.

At Eminabad, Bhai Lalo, a humble carpenter, welcomed Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana and invited them to stay at his place. During the stay with Bhai Lalo, Guru Nanak would spend time in an isolated area, laden with pebbles, known as ‘Rori’.

Amardeep Singh His purpose was to prepare himself, spiritually and physically, for the long and arduous journeys to be performed in the years to come. He reduced his diet, spent time in spiritual meditation and slept on pebbles in this place.

This gurdwara is an architectural marvel in which the brickwork has been done in a unique artistic style. The masons who constructed this building were admirably original and innovative. Gurmukhi characters formed by bricks on the arches convey Guru Nanak’s profound verses. Undivided Punjab’s largest annual Vaisakhi (harvest season) fair would be held on the grounds of this gurdwara, bustling with people of all faiths. This scenario changed after the partition of the subcontinent in 1947 which led to the migration of communities.

Amardeep Singh This Gurdwara Rori Sahib in Eminabad was made in the memory of Guru Nanak’s stay in the outskirts of the village, before he and Bhai Mardana embarked on their journey.

In 1993, the Pakistan Government resurrected the Rori Sahib Gurdwara and opened it for religious services.

We visit Gurdwara Bhai Lalo which is believed to have been built on the site where Bhai Lalo resided. This gurdwara was built during the reign of Maharajah Ranjit Singh. Post partition, its premises were used by migrants till the year 1993.

In Eminabad, a local feudal lord, Malik Bhago organised a ‘Brahm Bhoj’, the holy feast for the upper class people. Guru Nanak was also invited but he refused to attend the congregation. Malik Bhago’s ego was challenged. Infuriated, he asked Guru Nanak the reason for declining the invitation and preferring to be hosted by Bhai Lalo, the low caste carpenter.

Amardeep Singh Guru Nanak replied. The food he eats at Bhai Lalo’s place, tastes like milk because it’s been earned through hard work. But the food that he would eat at Malik Bhago’s place, would taste like blood because it’s been earned by violating the rights of the poor people.

aaise jan virale jag a(n)dhar parakh khajaanai paiaa || jaat varan te bhe ateetaa mamataa lobh chukaiaa || naam rate teerath se niramal dhukh haumai mail chukaiaa || naanak tin ke charan pakhaalai jinaa gurmukh saachaa bhaiaa ||

Raag Prabhati, Guru Nanak

In this world, those people are rare who have understood the treasure of self-reflection. Rising above caste and status, they get rid of worldly attachments and greed. Immersed in the purity of self-reflection, which is like a pilgrimage, they get rid of pain and filth of ego. Nanak says, he sacrifices himself to the spiritually oriented who have realized the Truth.

Raag Prabhati, Guru Nanak

In this materialistic world, such people are exceptional who have the wisdom to face the light of knowledge by giving up the darkness of ignorance.

This incident brings out the significant precept in Guru Nanak’s philosophy about social justice, human rights and integrity. He further expounded that a person is inferior or noble through deeds and not by simply being born in a family of a particular caste or religion.

hak paraiaa naanakaa us sooar us gai || gur peer haamaa taa bhare jaa muradhaar na khai ||

Raag Majh, Guru Nanak

Nanak says, to take what rightly belongs to others equates to Muslim eating pork and Hindu eating beef. Spiritual masters stand by those who do not eat the carcass of depriving others of their rights.

Raag Majh, Guru Nanak

In the strongest words, Guru Nanak says, depriving anyone of their due right is comparable to eating carcass. Emancipation can only be obtained by truthful living. Immoral acts cannot be justified or nullified by performing religious rituals.

Bhai Mardana expressed his wish to visit the shrine of Baba Sheikh Farid Shakarganj at Pakpattan.

From Eminabad Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana travelled through the cities of Makhdoom Pur Pahoran, Harappa, Burewala to reach Pakpattan.

Proceeding by foot and boat Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana halted at Tulamba, a town close to the banks of the River Ravi. This place is now known as Makhdoom Pur Pahoran.

We travel to Makhdoom Pur Pahoran to explore the memories of Guru Nanak’s visit to this town.

Amardeep Singh On the trade route from Lahore to Multan, Makhdoom Pur Pahoran was an important transit hub. Out here lived a person by the name of Sajjan who ran a guest house. Succumbing under materialistic temptations, he would rob and even kill his guests for their valuables.

We visit the erstwhile gurdwara, which is believed to have been built at the site where Sajjan ran his guest house. Presently, it is operating as the Govt. Higher Secondary School.

The name ‘Sajjan’ means, the noble one. Projecting himself as a pious man, Sajjan would dress like a Saint to hide his devious intentions. To attract commuters of all faiths, he had constructed a temple for Hindus and a mosque for the Muslims. To lure travellers he would provide free meals and accommodation. Guru Nanak, however, could see beyond Sajjan’s external appearances.

Amardeep Singh: Guru Nanak would beautifully transform people through the power of his verses. To make Sajjan Thug realize his mistake, he asked Bhai Mardana to play the Rabab, and the two sang, chaakareeaa cha(n)giaaieeaa avar siaanap kit || naanak naam samaal too(n) badhaa chhuTeh jit || (Raag Suhi, Guru Nanak) What is the worth of performing service, doing good and having intellect without proper conduct? Nanak says, contemplate to free the mind bound by negativities.

Raag Suhi, Guru Nanak

A noble person is the one who takes directions from the inner consciousness. Through the practice of introspection, the mind can be freed from the shackles of worldly desires.

Hearing these words, Sajjan Thug realized his misdeeds and began to conduct himself in accordance to the true meaning of his name, Sajjan, the noble one.

Amardeep Singh This gurdwara was built in the memory of the transformation of Sajjan Thug.

Reflecting on the transformation of Sajjan, I wonder, what are misdeeds?

manmukh kee mat kooR viaapee || bin har simaran paap sa(n)taapee ||

Raag Asa, Guru Nanak

The wayward mind’s intellect is engrossed in falsehood. Without contemplation it is tormented by misdeeds.

Raag Asa, Guru Nanak

Influenced by ego and greed, internal transgressions of moral values disturb the spiritual, emotional & physical equilibrium of oneself and that of the others. These transgressions are misdeeds.

Amardeep Singh In the outer periphery of this gurdwara, I am sitting in the classroom of a school that was built in the year 1917. Even though the founding community departed from these lands in the year 1947, it is so heartening to see that this school continues to run. The legacy of Guru Nanak to impart knowledge and wisdom continues to thrive.

kabeer meraa mujh meh kichh nahee jo kichh hai so teraa || teraa tujh kau saupate kiaa laagai meraa ||

Bhagat Kabir

Nothing is mine. All is Yours. Having surrendered to You what is Yours, then what is mine?

Bhagat Kabir

What a profound saying by Bhagat Kabir. “Nothing belongs to me. All that is, is Yours. And I submit it to you.” Look at the Will of the Almighty. The community that made this infrastructure for imparting wisdom and knowledge, has submitted it for the benefit of mankind.

From Makhdoom Pur Pahoran, Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana proceeded to Harappa.

We now travel to this historic town, famous for the excavated sites of the Indus Valley Civilization, dating back to 2000 BC. We visit the erstwhile Nanaksar Gurdwara, which is now being used as the premises for the Government Higher Secondary School.

Amardeep Singh From Tulamba, which is now known as Makhdoom Pur. Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana travelled northwards, along the River Ravi, either by boat or by foot and they reached the town of Harappa. This gurdwara was made in the memory of their arrival in Harappa.

ikOankaar sat gur prasaadh || Abchal Neev Dhari Gur Nanak, Nit Nit Charey Sawaye ||

One Omnipresent. The Truth. Realized through the Grace of Guidance. The unwavering foundation has been laid by Guru Nanak. Daily it grows manifold.

This foundation stone was laid by Sriman Sant Sangat Singh Ji Maharaj of Kamalia village.

After the partition of 1947, which resulted in the migration of communities, this site remained abandoned for years and was later put to good use, to impart education.

As I look at the students writing exams, I wonder if today’s competitive system, which instills fear, is beneficial for the long-term welfare of society?

agam agochar alakh apaaraa chi(n)taa karahu hamaaree || jal thal maheeal bharipur leenaa ghaT ghaT jot tum(h)aaree ||

Raag Bilawal, Guru Nanak

Inaccessible, Incomprehensible, Inexpressible and Infinite take care of my apprehensions. Within oceans, deserts and spaces resides the all-pervading, whose divinity is in every soul.

Raag Bilawal, Guru Nanak

Irrespective of the situation, one should make sincere efforts and not be influenced by the fear of uncertainty of outcomes.

As per local tradition, from Harappa, Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana visited Hazrat Baba Haji Sher’s Mausoleum in the outskirts of Burewala. Amardeep Singh: We are on our way to Burewala, where we will be meeting Baba Rafeeq Dukhi. He has been kind enough to agree to show us the sites of Baba Haji Sher and the gurdwara at Burewala.

Aazaan ke bagair || Aashiq namaaz padhde hai || Is ishq namaaz de vich sajnaa || Hath banne chadne nahi painde || Jado baitha saamne laal hove || Phir mathdhe banne nahi painde ||

Without a call for prayer, devotees recite within. In love for the beloved, they remain in prayers. They need not adhere to ritualistic protocols when the beloved is present within. Then physical bowing is not needed.

Amardeep Singh Salaam (Islamic greeting) Baba Ji. How are you?

Baba Rafeeq Dukhi Are you fine?

Amardeep Singh How about you?

Baba Rafeeq Dukhi All good.

Aashiq namaaz padhde, Aazaan ke bagair || Yeh raaz teri samajh me, shaayad naa aaegaa ||

Devotees recite within, without a call for prayer. This secret may not be understood by you.

Hazrat Baba Haji Sher was one of the earliest Muslim Saint in the subcontinent. He spent his life immersed in mysticism at this village where he lived till 730 AD.

Koi peer milaa mainun || Ali paak Muhammad da koi peer milaa mainun || Peeraan vichon peer mera iko hai vali || Panj naara panj tani iko naara Haidari ||

Grace me with a spiritual guide. The pure Ali and Prophet Muhammad, grace me with a spiritual guide. Amongst all spiritual guides, I only have one mentor. Five slogans and five spiritualists belong to only One.

At times mystical experiences cause spontaneous ecstatic moments in which experiencers lose control over themselves.

so kaheeaai dhevaanaa aap na pachhaaniee ||

Raag Maajh, Guru Nanak

Call them insane, who don’t understand themselves.

Raag Maajh, Guru Nanak

‘Deewana’ (insane) in Indic spiritual traditions is akin to insanity, a total loss of control over oneself. For Guru Nanak, the state of insanity is when one fails to recognize the Truth of self. The moment of ecstasy is when one experiences personal transformation.

Baba Rafeeq Dukhi Diwan Baba Haji Sher is the first Saint of ‘Bar-e-Sagheer’ (land of rivers). Guru Nanak visited this site to impart the message of universal fellowship. The message of Guru Nanak, which I admire, is to earn honestly and share generously. Our Saints also imparted this message. It is applicable in present times and will remain so in the future too.

Baba Rafeeq Dukhi walked us to the gurdwara, which was abandoned in the partition of 1947.

Amardeep Singh This gurdwara in the close proximity to the shrine of Hazrat Baba Haji Sher in Burewala was made in the memory of Guru Nanak’s visit.

Amardeep Singh Salaam (Islamic greeting) Baba Ji.

Badar Dewan Wasalaam (Islamic greeting).

Amardeep Singh Hope you are well.

Badar Dewan I am good. You tell about yourself and your family.

Amardeep Singh All well Baba Ji. It is a pleasure to meet you. Since when are you staying in Burewala?

Badar Dewan I am 85-86 years old. Our ancestors have been staying here. We have a memory of Baba Nanak at this place. When Nanak Sahib visited this place, he travelled the entire area. Before partition, 4-5 Sikh families lived here with us. Bhai Chettar Singh, Gurbaksh Singh, Datar Singh and Harnam Singh. They all used to stay here like our brothers. During the partition of 1947, they moved to Panipat & Karnal.

Amardeep Singh What do you think? Why did Guru Nanak visit this place?

Badar Dewan When he visited from Talwandi (Nankana Sahib) in district Sheikhupura. Where there is Nankana Sahib Darbar now in district Sheikhupura. When he arrived here, a question was posed to him. The Master has two ends. Which end should one catch and which end should one leave? Nanak Sahib says, hold truth and leave falsehood.

Trailing Guru Nanak’s journey, we now travel from Burewala to Nanak Tibba village in the outskirts of Pakpattan.

Amardeep Singh From Burewala, Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana travelled eastwards to Ajodhan, which is now known as Pakpattan. They stayed at this area, which was then a jungle and is now known as the village Nanak Tibba.

Sheikh Kamal, a disciple of Sheikh Ibrahim Farid Sani, was collecting wood in the forest when he heard Guru Nanak sing a hymn while Bhai Mardana played the Rabab.

too(n) aape aap varatadhaa aap banat banaiee || tudh bin dhoojaa ko nahee too rahiaa samaiee ||

Raag Malar, Guru Nanak

You yourself are all pervading and the Creator of the creation. Without you, there is no other. You permeate everywhere.

Raag Malar, Guru Nanak

Understanding the essence of the verse which implied that the entire world is a manifestation of one Omnipresent, Sheikh Kamal rushed to his master, Sheikh Ibrahim, to inform him about his experience of hearing Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana.

Sheikh Ibrahim, also known as Sheikh Brahm, was the 12th successor of Baba Sheikh Farid’s spiritual seat.

Sheikh Ibrahim warmly welcomed and hosted Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana, who thereafter stayed at Pakpattan for a few days.

We now visit the Mausoleum of Baba Farid within the city centre of Pakpattan.

Ganj-e-Shakkar Waliya Da Raja ||

Ganj-e-Shakkar (Baba Farid) is the King of Saints.

The city attracts a large number of devotees who visit to pay respect to the revered Chisti Saint.

Baba Mera Sabh Waliya Da Raja ||

My Baba (Farid) is the King of Saints.

Baba Sheikh Farid, the 13th century Sufi Saint of the Chisti order, is a towering figure of reverence. The ineffable sweetness of his words, kindness of his actions, warmth of unbiased service and generosity of an ocean, endowed him with the title of Ganj-e- Shakkar, meaning a treasure of sweetness.

At Baba Faird’s shrine, we were received by Diwan Shahbuddin Bakhtiyar Chisty.

Diwan Shahbuddin Bakhtiyar Chisty Bismillah-e-Rehman-e-Raheem (Islamic greeting). My name is Diwan Shahbuddin Bakhtiyar Chisty. I am from the progeny of Hazrat Baba Farid-ud-Din Masood Ganj-e-Shakkar. I am the 27th generation of the Sufi Saint. The era of Hazrat Baba Farid-ud-din, it starts from 1173 to 1266. The Sufi philosophy of the Saint is all about the Oneness of creation.

Amardeep Singh: And that exactly is the philosophy of Guru Nanak also because when he writes in his verse in Japji Sahib, he starts it with ‘Ik Ongkar’ (Guru Nanak’s doctrine). The numeral 1 signifies Oneness. And Baba Farid’s verses are also enshrined in the ‘Guru Granth Sahib’ (Sikh scripture), and as I am standing on his Mausoleum, I can read in Urdu. It is written, fareedhaa khaalak khalak meh khalak vasai rab maeh || ma(n)dhaa kis no aakheeaai jaa(n) tis bin koiee naeh ||

Sheikh Farid

Farid says, Creator is within creation, and the creation resides within the Creator. Who can be called negative when nothing is outside of the Creator’s manifestation.

Sheikh Farid

Amardeep Singh It is exactly the same.

Tera Pakpattan Subhan Allah || Baba Mera Sabh Waliya Da Raja ||

Your abode at Pakpattan is praiseworthy. The one who sees is blessed. My Baba (Farid) is the King of Saints.

Diwan Shahbuddin Bakhtiyar Chisty That is the shrine of Hazrat Baba Farid-ud-Din Masood Ganj-e-Shakkar.

Baba Mera Sabh Waliya Da Raja ||

My Baba (Farid) is the King of Saints.

Diwan Shahbuddin Bakhtiyar Chisty That is the shrine of Hazrat Baba Farid-ud-Din and that is the first ‘Shahzada Nasheen’ (son), Hazrat Badar-ud-Deen Sulaiman. That was his son.

fareedhaa sakar kha(n)dd nivaat guR maakhio maa(n)jhaa dhudh || sabhe vasatoo miTheeaa(n) rab na pujan tudh || fareedhaa roTee meree kaaTh kee laavan meree bhukh || jinaa khaadhee chopaRee ghane sahanige dhukh ||

Sheikh Farid

Farid says, grounded sugar, refined sugar, rock sugar, jaggery, honey and buffalo's milk. All are sweet but yet not equivalent to the sweetness of divinity. Farid says, though my bread is coarse, it satiates my hunger. Those who are engrossed in materialism, suffer in internal pain.

Sheikh Farid

True to his demeanour, Baba Sheikh Farid advises, if you have the power, stay humble. Share, even if you are a pauper. Do not utter even a word of harshness as the Divine resides in all.

Food is the intangible manifestation of love. The practice and institution of free food, ‘Langar’ (free community kitchen), was the fundamental constitutional principle of Baba Farid’s way of life, which became the hallmark of the culture of Punjab. Even today in Pakpattan, at every nook and corner, one can witness the tradition of ‘Langar’ (free community kitchen) institutionalized by him.

Amardeep Singh The tradition of ‘Langar’ (free community kitchen), the concept of ‘Wand Chakna’, to share amongst communities, is common amongst the saints of spiritual disposition. At Pakpattan, at Baba Farid’s ‘Darbar’ (shrine), I am humbled to be having ‘Langar’ (free community kitchen) amongst everyone here.

Baba Farid’s other important contribution is to the Punjabi literature. He used vernacular Punjabi as a language for his spiritual poetry, so the message could reach the masses.

This shrine resonates with renditions of Qawwals (Sufi Singers), who for generations have been transmitting the message of Sufi Masters to the masses through oral traditions.

Kaagaa sab tan khaaeeyo, moraa chun chun khaeeyo maas || Par do nainaa mat khaaeeyo, inhe piyaa milan ki aas ||

O crow, you may eat my entire body by pecking my flesh, but please leave the two eyes as they long to see the Divine.

Mehboob Fareed Khan We belong to the Jalandhar House of musicians. For over 700 years, our ancestors have been singing ‘Qawwali’ (Sufi rendition). When Pakistan was created, our elders came to Pakistan from India. As they had a soulful connection with Baba Farid. Our grandfather came here. At the Mausoleum of Baba Farid, there is no discrimination amongst people. The message of Oneness is for everyone. Seven hundred years have passed in imparting the message of humanity. People are still receiving the same guidance at this Mausoleum.

Farida turiaa turiaa jaa || Man vich val na paa ||

Farid says, keep moving towards unity. Farid says, do not keep any doubt in your heart.

Mehboob Fareed Khan Baba Farid and Baba Guru Nanak had a soulful connection. Baba Guru Nanak received this fragrance of love, peace and fellowship from Baba Farid. They both said humanity is the highest religion. Both promoted the same. So deep was Baba Farid-ud-Din ‘Sarkar’ (Master) and Baba Guru Nanak’s mutual love that Baba Farid’s verses were included in the ‘Guru Granth Sahib’ (Sikh scripture). I present to you one of the verses.

fareedhaa khaak na ni(n)dheeaai khaakoo jedd na koi || jeevadhiaa pairaa talai muiaa upar hoi ||

Sheikh Farid

Farid says, do not slander dust. Nothing is as great as the humble dust. When we are alive, it remains under our feet. When we are dead, it covers our head.

Sheikh Farid

Farida turiaa turiaa jaa || fareedhaa kaale maidde kapaRe kaalaa maiddaa ves || gunahee bhariaa mai firaa lok kahai dharaves || Farida turiaa turiaa jaa ||

Sheikh Farid

Farid says, keep moving towards unity. Farid says, my clothes are filthy and my conduct is filthy. Full of sins, I wander around. Yet people call me pious. Farid says, keep moving towards unity.

Sheikh Farid

Amardeep Singh For over 200 years, Baba Farid’s verses were transmitted through an oral tradition. When Guru Nanak was staying in Pakpattan, he collated his verses, which later on were enshrined in the ‘Guru Granth Sahib’ (Sikh scripture). The community later built this gurdwara at the Nanak Tibba village in Pakpattan, in the memory of Guru Nanak’s visit.

In departing from Pakpattan, what have I gathered from the lives Baba Farid and Guru Nanak?

tere dharasan kau ketee bilalai || viralaa ko cheenas gur sabadh milai ||

Raag Basant, Guru Nanak

To experience a glimpse of the Omnipresent many people lament. Rare are the ones who realize, it is through wisdom the all-pervading can be experienced.

Raag Basant, Guru Nanak

Many aspire to achieve the realization of Oneness but only a rare few walk the path.

For the conversation

Discussion Pointers

Episode 3: Ruhani Rawangi (Spiritual Departure)

The episode serves as a valuable resource for delving into the historical and philosophical dimensions of Guru Nanak's journey through Punjab. It highlights his meaningful interactions with various spiritual traditions, particularly the Sufi order exemplified by Baba Farid’s successors. These discussion points illuminate the interfaith dialogue and spiritual exchange that defined Guru Nanak's pursuit. Furthermore, they reveal the profound philosophical insights that transcend religious boundaries. Through this journey, Guru Nanak emerges as a spiritual teacher, social reformer, and spearhead of preserving and synthesizing wisdom across diverse traditions. His perspectives consistently emphasize the universal values of unity, honesty, generosity, and devotion, advocating for depth over the mere practice of ritualism.

Historical Discussion Pointers

1. What was the historical significance of Guru Nanak's departure from Sultanpur Lodhi in 1504 AD? The episode states that in 1504 AD, at the age of 35 years, Guru Nanak embarked on a journey to awaken the soul of society from the slumber of ignorance. Before departing, he visited his sister Bebe Nanaki, requesting her to look after his family while he was away. This journey marks the beginning of his spiritual odyssey to observe, assimilate, and disseminate the concept of 'Tawheed' or Oneness. How might this represent a pivotal moment that motivated Guru Nanak to embark on a spiritual quest?

2. What insights does the Malik Bhago incident provide about social dynamics in 16th-century Punjab? The episode describes a significant incident in Eminabad where a local feudal lord, Malik Bhago, organised a 'Brahm Bhoj', a holy feast for the upper-class people. Guru Nanak was also invited, but he refused to attend the congregation. When questioned about his preference for staying with the low caste carpenter Bhai Lalo, Guru Nanak replied that he preferred simple food earned through hard work, like that provided by Bhai Lalo, which tastes as sweet as milk, over a lavish feast obtained by violating the rights of the underprivileged, such as the food served by Malik Bhago, which tastes like blood. How does this incident illuminate the social stratification, economic exploitation, and religious hypocrisy prevalent during Guru Nanak's time?

3. What historical significance does the transformation of Sajjan Thug hold in understanding Guru Nanak's approach to social reform? In Makhdoom Pur Pahoran (Tulamba), Guru Nanak encountered Sajjan, who ran a guest house but would rob and even kill his guests for their valuables. Despite projecting himself as pious and having constructed both a temple and a mosque to attract travelers, Sajjan's true nature was recognized by Guru Nanak, who transformed him through the power of his verses. How does this historical account demonstrate Guru Nanak's method of inspiring a positive change through spiritual awakening rather than reprimanding?

4. How did Guru Nanak's interaction with the Sufi tradition at Pakpattan reflect interfaith dialogues in medieval India? The episode describes Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana's visit to Pakpattan, where they met Sheikh Kamal and Sheikh Ibrahim (the 12th successor of Baba Sheikh Farid). Sheikh Ibrahim warmly welcomed and hosted Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana, who thereafter stayed at Pakpattan for a few days. This interaction with the Chisti Sufi order demonstrates the open communication between different spiritual traditions. How did these interfaith interactions contribute to Guru Nanak's understanding and articulation of universal principles?

5. What role did Guru Nanak play in preserving and disseminating Baba Farid's philosophical views? Mehboob Fareed Khan, a descendant of traditional Qawwali musicians, notes that Baba Farid and Baba Guru Nanak had a soulful connection and so deep was Baba Farid-ud-Din 'Sarkar' (Master) and Baba Guru Nanak's mutual love that Baba Farid's verses were included in the 'Guru Granth Sahib' (Sikh scripture). The episode further states that for over 200 years, Baba Farid's verses were transmitted through an oral tradition. When Guru Nanak was staying in Pakpattan, he collated his verses, which later on were enshrined in the 'Guru Granth Sahib'. How significant was Guru Nanak's role in preserving these spiritual insights, and what does this tell us about his approach to religious inclusivity?

6. How did the historical practice of Langar influence Guru Nanak's community- building approach? The episode describes the practice of Langar (free community kitchen) as the intangible manifestation of love and the fundamental constitutional principle of Baba Farid's way of life, which became the hallmark of the culture of Punjab. It notes that even today, at every nook and corner, one can witness the tradition of 'Langar' institutionalized by him. How did this pre-existing tradition influence Guru Nanak's approach to building inclusive spiritual communities, and how did it come to be associated so strongly with Sikh identity in later periods?

Philosophical Discussion Pointers:

1. How does Guru Nanak's response to Malik Bhago reflect his philosophy on honest work versus exploitation? Guru Nanak's statement that food earned through hard work tastes like milk, while food earned through exploitation tastes like blood encapsulates a profound philosophical stance. This is reinforced by the verse: Nanak says, to take what rightly belongs to others equates to Muslim eating pork and Hindu eating beef. Spiritual masters stand by those who do not eat the carcass of depriving others of their rights. How does this powerful metaphor express Guru Nanak's ethical philosophy regarding honest labor, exploitation, and the moral status of ill-gotten wealth?

2. What does Guru Nanak's verse to Sajjan Thug reveal about his understanding of true service and introspection? When confronting Sajjan Thug, Guru Nanak sang: What is the worth of performing service, doing good and having intellect without proper conduct? Nanak says, contemplate to free the mind bound by negativities. The episode explains that a noble person is one who takes direction from the inner consciousness. Through the practice of introspection, the mind can be freed from the shackles of worldly desires. How does this philosophical position challenge superficial religiosity and emphasize authentic inner transformation?

3. How does the concept of "Deewana" (insane) in Guru Nanak's philosophy provide insights into spiritual consciousness? The episode mentions that at times mystical experiences cause spontaneous ecstatic moments in which experiencers lose control over themselves and quotes Guru Nanak: Call them insane, who don't understand themselves. It further explains that 'Deewana' (insane) in Indic spiritual traditions is akin to insanity, a total loss of control over oneself. For Guru Nanak, the state of insanity is when one fails to recognize the Truth of self. The moment of ecstasy is when one experiences personal transformation. How does this reconceptualization of "insanity" challenge conventional understanding of spiritual awareness?

4. How do the messages of Baba Farid and Guru Nanak converge on the concept of universal fellowship and Oneness? Diwan Shahbuddin Bakhtiyar Chisty, a 27th generation descendant of Baba Farid, explains that the Sufi philosophy of the Saint is all about the Oneness of creation. The narrator responds by noting that exactly is the philosophy of Guru Nanak also because when he writes in his verse in Japji Sahib, he starts it with 'Ik Ongkar' (Guru Nanak's doctrine). The numeral 1 signifies Oneness. How does this shared emphasis on Oneness transcend religious boundaries and provide a foundation for universal fellowship?

5. What philosophical significance does the practice of "Wand Chakna" (sharing) hold in both Sufi and Sikh traditions? The episode quotes Baba Rafeeq Dukhi saying that the message of Guru Nanak, which he admires, is to earn honestly and share generously. Later, the narrator notes that the tradition of 'Langar' (free community kitchen), the concept of 'Wand Chakna', to share amongst communities, is common amongst the saints of spiritual disposition. How does this shared ethical principle of generous sharing reflect deeper philosophical understandings about human interconnectedness and the proper relationship with material wealth?

6. How does Baba Farid's verse about dust reflect the philosophical principle of humility shared by both traditions? The episode includes Baba Farid's verse, which highlights not to slander dust, as nothing is as great as the humble dust. When we are alive, it remains under our feet; when we are dead, it covers our head. This powerful metaphor uses the common element of dust to impart profound lessons about humility and the human condition. How does this philosophical perspective on humility align with Guru Nanak's philosophy, and what does it reveal about the shared ethical values between these spiritual traditions?

7. What insights does the episode provide about Guru Nanak's approach to religious orthodoxy and ritualism? Throughout his journey, Guru Nanak encounters and challenges various forms of ritualistic practice, from Malik Bhago's ‘holy feast’ to Sajjan's superficial piety. The episode also quotes verses emphasizing that immoral acts cannot be justified or nullified by performing religious rituals. Similarly, it includes a Sufi exposition that without a call for prayer, devotees recite within. In love for the beloved, they remain in prayers. They need not adhere to ritualistic protocols when the beloved is present within. How do these perspectives reflect a shared critique of empty ritualism in favor of authentic spiritual connection?

8. How does the episode articulate the relationship between spiritual wisdom and the realization of Oneness? The episode concludes with Guru Nanak's verse: To experience a glimpse of the Omnipresent, many people lament. Rare are the ones who realize that it is through wisdom that the all-pervading can be experienced. It then observes: Many aspire to achieve the realization of Oneness, but only a rare few walk the path. What does this suggest about the philosophical relationship between intellectual understanding, spiritual practice, and the ultimate realization of Oneness that lies at the heart of both Guru Nanak's and Baba Farid's philosophy?

YouTube captions — spoken narration only (no speaker labels or on-screen verses). Click a timestamp to open the video at that point.

0:00:05‘Ruhani Rawangi’, ‘Spiritual Departure’, presents the onset of the keen beholder's first odyssey to observe, assimilate and disseminate ‘Tawheed’, Oneness. Guru Nanak intermingles with mystics at the seat of ‘Shakarganj’.

0:02:34In 1504 AD, at the age of 35 years, Guru Nanak embarks on a journey to awaken the soul of society from the slumber of ignorance. from the slumber of ignorance. Before leaving Sultanpur Lodhi, Guru Nanak visits his sister Bebe Nanaki, requesting her to look out for his family while he is away.

0:03:00With the concurrence of Bebe Nanaki and his wife, Mata Sulakhni, Guru Nanak, along with Bhai Mardana depart from Sultanpur Lodhi to Saidpur, a city which is now known as Eminabad. We now travel from Sultanpur Lodhi in India to visit Eminabad in Pakistan to explore Guru Nanak's narrative in this city.

0:03:51At Eminabad, Bhai Lalo, a humble carpenter, welcomed Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana and invited them to stay at his place. During the stay with Bhai Lalo, Guru Nanak would spend time in an isolated area, laden with pebbles, known as 'Rori'. His purpose was to prepare himself, spiritually and physically, for the long and the arduous journeys to be performed in the years to come.

0:04:29He reduced his diet, spent time in spiritual meditation and slept on pebbles in this place. This Gurdwara is an architectural marvel in which the brickwork has been done in a unique artistic style.

0:04:55The masons who constructed this building were admirably original and innovative. Gurmukhi characters formed by bricks on the arches convey Guru Nanak's profound verses. Undivided Punjab's largest annual Vaisakhi fair (harvest season) would be held on the grounds of this gurdwara, bustling with people of all faiths.

0:05:24This scenario changed after the partition of the subcontinent in 1947, which led to the migration of communities. This Gurdwara Rori Sahib in Eminabad was made in the memory of Guru Nanak's stay in the outskirts of the village, before he and Bhai Mardana embarked on their journey.

0:05:57In 1993, the Pakistan Government resurrected the Rori Sahib Gurdwara and opened it for religious services. We visit Gurdwara Bhai Lalo, which is believed to have been built on the site where Bhai Lalo resided.

0:06:31This gurdwara was built during the reign of Maharaja Ranjit Singh. Post partition, its premises were used by migrants till the year 1993. In Eminabad, a local feudal lord, Malik Bagho, organized a ‘Brahm Bhoj’, the holy feast for the upper class people. Guru Nanak was also invited but he refused to attend the congregation.

0:07:03Malik Bhago’s ego was challenged. Infuriated, he asked Guru Nanak the reason for declining the invitation and preferring to be hosted by Bhai Lalo, the low caste carpenter. Guru Nanak replied, the food that he eats at Bhai Lalo's place, tastes like milk because it's been earned through hard work.

0:07:32But the food that he would eat at Malik Bhago’s place, would taste like blood because it's been earned by violating the rights of the poor people. In this materialistic world,

0:08:59such people are exceptional who have the wisdom to face the light of knowledge by giving up the darkness of ignorance. This incident brings out the significant precept in Guru Nanak's philosophy about social justice, human rights and integrity. He further expounded that a person is inferior or noble through deeds and not by simply being born in a family of a particular caste or religion.

0:10:13In the strongest words, Guru Nanak says, depriving anyone of their due right is comparable to eating carcass. Emancipation can only be obtained by truthful living. Immoral acts cannot be justified or nullified by performing religious rituals. Bhai Mardana expressed his wish to visit the shrine of Baba Sheikh Farid Shakarganj at Pakpattan.

0:10:45From Eminabad, Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana travelled through the cities of Makhdoompur Pahoran, Harappa, Burewala, to reach Pakpattan. Proceeding by foot and boat, Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana halted at Tulamba, a town close to the banks of the River Ravi. This place is now known as Makhdoopur Pahoran.

0:11:14We travel to Makhdoompur Pahoran to explore the memories of Guru Nanak's visit to this town. On the trade route from Lahore to Multan, Makhdoompur Pahoran was an important transit hub.

0:11:58Out here, lived a person by the name of Sajjan who ran a guesthouse. Succumbing under materialistic temptations, he would rob and even kill his guests for their valuables. We visit the erstwhile gurdwara, which is believed to have been built at the site where Sajjan ran his guest house.

0:12:27Presently, it is operating as the Government Higher Secondary School. The name Sajjan means, the noble one.

0:12:54Projecting himself as a pious man, Sajjan would dress like a Saint to hide his devious intentions. To attract commuters of all faiths, he had constructed a temple for Hindus and a mosque for the Muslims. To lure travelers he would provide free meals and accommodation. Guru Nanak, however, could see beyond Sajjan’s external appearances.

0:13:25Guru Nanak would beautifully transform people through the power of his verses. To make Sajjan Thug realize his mistake, he asked Bhai Mardana to play the Rabab and the two sang. A noble person is the one

0:14:10who takes directions from the inner consciousness. Through the practice of introspection, the mind can be freed from the shackles of worldly desires. Hearing these words, Sajjan Thug realized his misdeeds and began to conduct himself in accordance to the true meaning of his name, Sajjan, a noble one.

0:14:33This gurdwara was built in the memory of the transformation of Sajjan Thug. Reflecting on the transformation of Sajjan, I wonder, what are misdeeds? Influenced by ego and greed, internal transgressions of moral values disturb the spiritual, emotional and physical equilibrium of oneself and that of the others.

0:15:19These transgressions are misdeeds. In the outer periphery of this gurdwara, I am sitting in the classroom of a school that was built in the year 1917. Even though the founding community departed from these lands in the year 1947, it is so heartening to see that this school continues to run. The legacy of Guru Nanak to impart knowledge and wisdom continues to thrive.

0:16:05What a profound saying by Bhagat Kabir. Nothing belongs to me. All that is, is Yours and I submit it to You. Look at the will of the Almighty. The community that made this infrastructure for imparting wisdom and knowledge, has submitted it for the benefit of mankind.

0:16:37From Makhdoompur Pahoran, Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana proceeded to Harappa. We now travel to this historic town, famous for the excavated sites of the Indus Valley civilization, dating back to 2000 B.C.

0:17:10We visit the erstwhile Nanaksar Gurdwara, which is now being used as the premises for the Government Higher Secondary School. From Tulamba, which is now known as Makhdoompur, Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana travelled northwards, along the River Ravi, either by boat or by foot and they reached the town of Harappa.

0:17:49This gurdwara was made in the memory of their arrival in Harappa. This foundation stone was laid by Sriman Sant Sangat Singh Ji Maharaj of Kamalia village.

0:18:19After the partition of 1947, which resulted in the migration of communities, this site remained abandoned for years and was later put to good use, to impart education. As I look at the students writing exams, I wonder if today's competitive system, which instills fear, is beneficial for the long term welfare of society?

0:19:50Irrespective of the situation, one should make sincere efforts and not be influenced by the fear of uncertainty of outcomes. As per local tradition, from Harappa, Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana visited Hazrat Baba Haji Sher’s Mausoleum in the outskirts of Burewala.

0:20:26We are on our way to Burewala, where we will be meeting Baba Rafeeq Dukhi. He has been kind enough to agree to show us the sites of Baba Haji Sher and the gurdwara at Burewala. Hazrat Baba Haji Sher was one of the earliest Muslim Saint in the subcontinent.

0:21:40He spent his life immersed in mysticism at this village where he lived till 730 AD. At times mystical experiences cause spontaneous ecstatic moments in which experiencers lose control over themselves.

0:22:36'Deewana'(insane) in Indic spiritual traditions is akin to insanity, a total loss of control over oneself. For Guru Nanak, the state of insanity is when one fails to recognize the Truth of self. The moment of ecstasy is when one experiences personal transformation.

0:23:34Baba Rafeeq Dukhi walked us to the gurdwara, which was abandoned in the partition of 1947. This gurdwara, in the close proximity to the shrine of Baba Haji Sher in Burewala

0:24:20was made in the memory of Guru Nanak's visit. Trailing Guru Nanak's journey, We now travel from Burewala

0:26:06to Nanak Tibba village in the outskirts of Pakpattan. From Burewala, Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana traveled eastwards to Ajodhan, which is now known as Pakpattan. They stayed at this area, which then was a jungle and is now known as the village Nanak Tibba.

0:26:35Sheikh Kamal, a disciple of Sheikh Ibrahim Farid Sani, was collecting wood in the forest when he heard Guru Nanak sing a hymn while Bhai Mardana played the Rabab. Understanding the essence of the verse

0:27:43which implied that the entire world is a manifestation of one Omnipresent, Sheikh Kamal rushed to his master, Sheikh Ibrahim, to inform him about his experience of hearing Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana. Sheikh Ibrahim, also known as Sheikh Brahm, was the 12th successor of Baba Sheikh Farid's spiritual seat.

0:28:12Sheikh Ibrahim warmly welcomed and hosted Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana, who thereafter stayed at Pakpattan for a few days. We now visit the Mausoleum of Baba Farid within the city center of Pakpattan. The city attracts a large number of devotees who visit to pay respect to the revered Chisti Saint.

0:28:58Baba Sheikh Farid, the 13th century Sufi Saint of the Chisti order, is a towering figure of reverence. The ineffable sweetness of his words, kindness of his actions, warmth of unbiased service and generosity of an ocean, endowed him with the title of Ganj-e-Shakkar, meaning a treasure of sweetness.

0:29:26At Baba Farid's shrine, we were received by Diwan Shahbuddin Bakhtiyar Chisty. My name is Diwan Shahbuddin Bakhtiyar Chisty. I am from the progeny of Hazrat Baba Farid-ud-Din Masood Ganj-e-Shakkar. I am the 27th generation of the Sufi Saint. The era of Hazrat Baba Faird-ud-Din, starts from 1173 to 1266.

0:29:53The Sufi philosophy of the Saint is all about Oneness of creation. Exactly. And that is the philosophy of Guru Nanak also because when he writes in his verse in Japji Sahib, he starts with ‘Ik Ongkar’ (Guru Nanak’s doctrine). The numeral one signifies Oneness. And Baba Farid's verses are also enshrined in the 'Guru Granth Sahib' (scripture), and as I am standing at his Mausoleum, I can read in Urdu.

0:30:20It is written. That is the shrine of Hazrat Baba Farid-ud-Din Masood Ganj-e-Shakkar.

0:31:16<b>That is the shrine of </b> <b>Hazrat Baba Farid-ud-Din</b> <b>and that is the first ‘Shahzada Nasheen’ (son),</b> <b>Hazrat Badr-ud-Din Suleman.</b> <b>That was his son.</b> True to his demeanour, Baba Sheikh Farid advises,

0:32:44if you have the power, stay humble. Share, even if you are a pauper. Do not utter even a word of harshness as the Divine resides in all. Food is the intangible manifestation of love.

0:33:15The practice and institution of free food, 'Langar' was the fundamental constitutional principle of Baba Farid's way of life, which became the hallmark of the culture of Punjab. Even today in Pakpattan, at every nook and corner, one can witness the tradition of 'Langar' (free community kitchen) institutionalized by him.

0:33:46The tradition of 'Langar' (free community kitchen), the concept of 'Wand Chakna', to shared amongst communities, is common amongst the saints of spiritual disposition. At Pakpattan, at Baba Farid's Darbar (shrine), I am humbled to be having 'Langar' (free community kitchen) amongst everyone here. Baba Farid's other important contribution is to the Punjabi literature.

0:34:16He used vernacular Punjabi as a language for his spiritual poetry, so the message could reach the masses. This shrine resonates with renditions of 'Qawalas' (Sufi Singers), who for generations have been transmitting the message of Sufi masters to the masses through oral traditions.

0:38:40For over 200 years, Baba Farid's verses were being transmitted through an oral tradition. When Guru Nanak was staying in Pakpattan, he collated his verses, which later on were enshrined in the 'Guru Granth Sahib' (scripture). The community later built this Gurdwara at the Tibba village in Pakpattan, in the memory of Guru Nanak's visit.

0:39:08In departing from Pakpattan, what have I gathered from the lives of Baba Farid and Guru Nanak? Many aspire to achieve the realization of Oneness, but only a very few walk the path.

04 Paschimi Surya Uday Sunrise in the West Evoking rationality, “to awaken the mind from the slumber of ignorance.” 37:15›
▶ Watch ↓ Script PDF

‘Paschimi Surya Uday’, ‘Sunrise in the West’, presents the pragmatic maven’s exemplifications to awaken the mind from the slumber of ignorance. Guru Nanak evokes rationality in ‘Uttarkaushal’.

paRiaa boojhai so paravaan || jis sir dharageh kaa neesaan ||

Raag Dhanasri, Guru Nanak

The ones who read and thereon understand are worthy. On them, the presence of supreme wisdom is marked.

Raag Dhanasri, Guru Nanak

Learning is the theoretical part of a concept and understanding the practical component. Guru Nanak’s journeys intertwine both these aspects and provide a repository of valuable insights for effective assimilation of knowledge.

Punjab, the land of five rivers in the Indus belt has been a melting pot of diverse cultures, where Central Asian Islamic and Gangetic Hindu cultures intertwined.

Departing from Pakpattan in the Indus region, Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana proceeded east towards the Gangetic region. They crossed River Sutlej and travelled through Sirsa, Karah, Pehowa and Kurukshetra to reach Haridwar. They first visited the city of Sirsa.

In present times, due to political reasons, the India-Pakistan border can only be crossed at a few designated points. Hence, to retrace Guru Nanak’s footsteps, we visit India through Wagah border and continue our journey from Sirsa.

Located at the edge of the Thar Desert, Sirsa is a historic city, believed to have been established in the 3rd century AD by King Saras. We visit Gurdwara Chilla Sahib made in the memory of Guru Nanak’s visit to Sirsa.

The oral narrative at this site, which finds no mention in the earliest ‘Janamsakhis’, is that at Sirsa, Guru Nanak visited a local religious fair organized by Fakirs. His attire made it difficult for people to identify him neither as Muslim nor as Hindu. Confused, when the Fakirs inquired about his religious background, Guru Nanak smiled and said, if he is seen as a human, then disparity based on religion, caste and creed can be dispelled.

jaanahu jot na poochhahu jaatee aagai jaat na he ||

Raag Asa, Guru Nanak

Recognize the universal light of unity and do not inquire about lineage. In Divinity there is no disparity.

Raag Asa, Guru Nanak

Guru Nanak, saw the ‘Jyot’, the universal light residing in everyone. Once this knowledge is kindled, it illuminates the internal religion, which sees no difference between you, me and the Creator.

From Sirsa, Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana travelled to village Karah in the outskirts of Pehowa town.

In Karah village, we visit the historic gurdwara built by Raja Udai Singh of Kaithal State to commemorate the visit of Guru Nanak.

Interestingly, the narrative on the board of this gurdwara states that in this village, Guru Nanak brought to life a dead camel and made it have a discourse with a local Pandit to shatter his ego. This narrative does not find mention in the earliest ‘Janamsakhis’.

jo usaare so ddaahasee tis bin avar na koi ||

Raag Ramkali, Guru Nanak

The Omnipresent energy that creates, also destroys. Apart from it, there is none else.

Raag Ramkali, Guru Nanak

One who creates, destroys too. Similarly, the one in ego, can shed it too, through one’s own efforts.

I feel, through dialogue, Guru Nanak would have revived the divinity of the Pandit. The oral narrative is probably a metaphoric way of conveying a more profound import. The dead camel may be representing Pandit’s spiritually dead consciousness which is recovered through Guru Nanak’s words of wisdom.

From Karah, Guru Nanak and Bhai Mardana travelled to Pehowa, an ancient town from the era of the epic battle of Mahabharat.

We now visit the historic Hindu religious site on the edge of ‘Sannihit Sarovar’, a pond believed to hold sacred waters of the now extinct Saraswati River, which is said to have once existed in this region.

Amardeep Singh In the Hindu Puranic texts, the city of Pehowa is mentioned as a place of religious significance.

People visit Pehowa to bathe in the ‘Sannihit Sarovar’ with a belief that the holy water gives peace to the departed souls, washes the sins of an individual and appeases the Creator. It is also famous for ‘Pind Daan’, a ritual for the salvation of the deceased. ‘Pind’ are ritualistic offerings of balls made of rice flour, wheat flour, dried milk, honey and sesame seeds, which are used by the priest to perform prayers with an expectation of receiving ‘Daan’ - a charitable payment.

Watching innocent people in hope of salvation being influenced and misguided by the priestly class, Guru Nanak sang in antipathy, kooR bol muradhaar khai || avaree no samajhaavan jai || muThaa aap muhaae saathai || naanak aaisaa aagoo jaapai ||

Raag Majh, Guru Nanak

Speaking false words is akin to eating the carcass. Those who utter falsehood, preach to others. Themselves deceived, they deceive others. Nanak says, such people are perceived as leaders.

Raag Majh, Guru Nanak

Spiritual, social and political affairs influence society. An honourable leader, with positive qualities, blended with divinity, has the ability to uplift the community. However, an astray leader impedes critical thinking, misguides the innocent, and thus causes social and political unrest. Guru Nanak relentlessly continued to spark the spirit of rational thinking by bringing to notice the shallowness of dogmatic practices and the hypocrisy of leaders. Observing people throng to places designated as religiously important to perform rituals, I wonder, if it is so simple a path to attain redemption?

alakh apaar apaar saachaa aap maar milaieeaai || haumai mamataa lobh jaalahu sabadh mail chukaieeaai ||

Raag Bilawal, Guru Nanak

The all-pervading energy is inexpressible, infinite, illimitable and true. It is experienced by eliminating self-conceit. When egotistical attachment and greed are burnt, wisdom removes the filth of discontentment.

Raag Bilawal, Guru Nanak

Only those who eliminate vices within, can achieve divinity.

Located on the banks of ‘Sannihit Sarovar’ is Gurdwara Sheesh Mahal, built in the memory of Guru Nanak’s visit to Pehowa.
